item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, this is a veritable small child shouting at a Titan here, but I actually think the named Foundation personnel here are out of character. The Foundation is meant to be cold and calculating. It insists on numbers. Violent subjects are restrained. The containment protocols take first priority, even above research.

Just feels like these staff are getting too involved to realistically be allowed to remain on the same project by the powers that be.

item.title: Re: Question(s) for soulless
item.content:encoded: Kindness is Dr. K███ M██ (the black boxes hide "Kind Man")

item.title: Question(s) for soulless
item.content:encoded: upon reading this again i wanted to ask, what role did "kindness" play in this… things creation? Is kindness the eldritch? for what I've read from the other articles tagged with… it. It seems that it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I return to read two and a half years later—forgetting I've read it once already—and I find my past self has commented precisely what my current self thinks. Nice to know I'm consistent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the Mind-Affectation Resistance Scale. I'm going to be using it a lot. stealing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this guy, he's poetic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quoting the skip's creator from earlier in this thread:

If spread through enough people, the entity will disperse itself into the minds of millions/billions of humans, causing a complete restructuring of how humans percieve and understand music.

The bolded-out part doesn't seem to be present in the description of the anomalous effects. Am I missing something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Music is the creation of people - Jayden believes that the music are the /children/ of the people. But the people abandon their children (music) easily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for the necro… but I do have a question.

I'm not sure about the relationship between music and the concept of children. Or are the 'children' the music albums that are rarely heard and not appreciated?

Apart from this, though the skip (writing) is a bit confusing, and the skip (aka Jayden) a bit annoying, I like it. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2992's secondary effects include changes of behavior, such as the increased tolerance for different genres of music and a strong desire to seek more obscure music to listen to and suggest to others.

Huh, didn't know I was infected by a skip. Huh.

*goes back to listening to minimal techno built out of samples of Tuvan throat singing and Jimi Hendrix guitar solos*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit wordy and purple for my taste, but first 2900 SCP I upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Lovecraft the bard sang unflattering stories about their kind, but the bard sings all the songs he is paid to sing even if they aren't the truth, isn't that right?

Interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really dislike the floweriness of the man's speech - made it really hard to read through the logs - but I like the rest of it sufficiently for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it as a draft, and I still do. Even without considering the big implications, I like what is stated outright.

For added creepiness factor: The memetic effect spreads via online playlists. How often do you listen to playlists curated by others? I know I do almost daily…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sometimes, the crazy are right. At least we know what to do with hipsters now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lovecraftian oracle in the form of a DJ. Didn't think this could work, but you pulled it off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The best interview logs I've read on this site bar none. Great concept, too. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*shrug*

I imply a lot of things in this piece. That might be one of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Several minutes after I read this, I suddenly realized something terrifying.

SCP-2992's primary effect triggers when a given subject listens to one or more suggested songs. Once affected, a given subject will perceive the continuous playing of previously heard music. This continuous playing is interrupted only when the given subject listens to new music.

Having a song stuck inside your head is caused by an Eldritch Abomination infecting your brain! If this is what you were trying to imply, that the seemingly mundane phenomenon of having a song stuck in your head is really caused by this thing, then you did it brilliantly.

Please tell me, I have know. Were you really trying to imply that I am infected because I have a song stuck in my head? And that everybody who has a song stuck in their head is infected? Or was this accidental?

It is kind of nice to be able to blame something other than myself for getting a silly song about a boy asking a girl if he can [DATA EXPUNGED] stuck in my head for the last few months.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't have this problem at all with this scp, although I did have it with the other one you mentioned, although it might just be because I didn't read it very well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ..is..is this a starving Infovore in the vein of Charles Strauss's "Laundry" series? Or at least inspired by?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow…this gave me the shivers. Really good show! Nice to see a Lovecraft SCiP done right. Upvote gladly given.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All I could think reading the interviews is that he talks like a music-themed Saturday-morning cartoon villain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If spread through enough people, the entity will disperse itself into the minds of millions/billions of humans, causing a complete restructuring of how humans percieve and understand music.

And the Foundation was letting it spread willy nilly because they were too busy dealing with a purposefully obtuse DJ to notice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For me this has a similar problem to SCP-2442. The execution of the concept makes it hard to tell what's going on, and is so vague that I could see it being other things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I said in chat, it feels a bit heavy handed, and some of the prose is pretty thick, but I liked it. I can imagine how those abandoned, neglected albums that were forgotten by time would feel if they were sentient.

Also is this skip implying that hipsters are a result of an anomaly because if it is then that one entry in the List of Things Dr. Bright Is Not Allowed To Do would be correct

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My take on this: Soulless liked musical Fifthism and wants moar.

In any case, this is cool and deserves my upvote, even if I wholeheartedly believe that those songs will eventually learn to look for love somewhere else, growing and maturing and leaving their obsession with a parent's abandonment. If humanity forgot about them, maybe we don't really deserve them back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this is dangerous not only because its in uncontained, but if spread to enough people, either the entity will be released into the physical world or people will eventually learn of the anomalous and society will collapse?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+Le spoilers
-Le spoiled

Based on your spoilers in the forums, I think I figured it out. The Lovecraftian entity, 2992-1, really, REALLY likes human music, and tried to show its fandom the only way a Lovecraftian entity can: by breaking some poor bastard's (2992's) mind. Then both decided that they needed to spread the joy. The memetic effect is the REAL SCP, and, as with real world memes, it's spreading virally, but the Foundation derp'd, so now all they have is a former DJ locked in a quiet room, while a Lovecraftian music lover roams freely in the minds of who knows how many people.

Edit: The author beat me to it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The final interview log implies the effect is both extremely Virulent and pretty much completely uncontained, having spread wildly without Foundation notice. The guy goes so far as to claim that all music is somehow involved in the spread of its Memetic effects.

In simpler terms, the final interview log has such large implications that the Foundation is pending Keter reclassification until it can confirm or deny these claims. Since the only way they can confirm is the SCP itself, the Foundation is treating this with an arm twisted behind its back, so to speak.

Is this clear? Should I try to clarify this further in the interview log? I tried to put it all in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not seeing how this Keter.

item.title: Obligated Author Commentary
item.content:encoded:
ca·dence
/ˈkādns/
noun
noun: cadence; plural noun: cadences

2.
Music
a sequence of notes or chords comprising the close of a musical phrase.
"the final cadences of the Prelude"
